APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (natural language understanding / sequence tagging) and proposes a neural attention-based model (K-SAN) that incorporates external knowledge in an end-to-end manner, which is squarely within top-tier conference topics. The methodology (knowledge-guided encodings + attention + RNN-GRU tagger), the evaluation on standard ATIS splits with F-measure, and comparisons to strong baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) match the methods and standards expected at these venues. The work is targeted to the right audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in structured knowledge, attention mechanisms, and NLU) and is positioned appropriately with thorough related work coverage. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic (neural sequence tagging/SLU) and proposes a knowledge-guided attention architecture (K-SAN) that is directly relevant to the venue’s audience. It uses methods and evaluation standards expected at top conferences (end-to-end neural models, attention, comparisons to strong baselines, F-measure on ATIS) and reports hyperparameters and thorough related work. Given the focus on improving slot filling with external structured knowledge and empirical comparisons to state-of-the-art taggers, the paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates K-SAN on ATIS and compares against several strong baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN), which supports the core performance claims but is limited to a single dataset. The summary does not report explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of the attention/knowledge components, nor a discussion of the method's own failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and hyperparameters are reported aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and cross-dataset validation is missing, so the experimental evaluation is adequate but omits important analyses. | B(3): The experiments evaluate K-SAN on the ATIS benchmark and compare against several strong baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN), and hyperparameters are reported, which supports the main performance claims. However, the evaluation appears limited to one domain/dataset (ATIS) and the summary does not mention explicit ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of the attention/knowledge components. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the paper does not discuss its own failure cases or limitations, and code availability is not mentioned, reducing reproducibility despite reported hyperparameters. Overall, the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing important analyses to fully support all claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates K-SAN on ATIS and compares against several strong baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN), which supports the core performance claims but is limited to a single dataset. Overall, the empirical evaluation is adequate but missing important analyses to fully support all claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a novel architecture (K-SAN) that integrates general prior knowledge as guidance via structural attention in an end-to-end neural sequence tagger, which is presented as a new way to combine heterogeneous external knowledge (dependency paths, AMR, knowledge-graph relations) with RNNs. This clearly differentiates it from prior feature-enrichment and tree-based models (e.g., Tree-RNN) and constitutes a non-trivial extension by learning attention over knowledge-derived substructures and feeding a knowledge-guided sentence representation to an RNN-GRU tagger. The demonstrated efficiency and improved generalization on ATIS further support that the approach is meaningfully new rather than a minor combination of known techniques. | B(4): The paper proposes K-SAN, which (per the summary) is the first neural approach to incorporate general prior knowledge (e.g., dependency paths or AMR subgraphs) via an attention mechanism in an end-to-end model, clearly differentiating it from standard RNN/Tree-RNN/CNN baselines. Encoding knowledge-guided substructures, computing attention over them, and feeding the resulting representation to an RNN-GRU is a non-trivial extension beyond existing sequence taggers, and would likely provide new insight to experts working on slot-filling despite the underlying task being well-studied.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a novel architecture (K-SAN) that integrates general prior knowledge as guidance via structural attention in an end-to-end neural sequence tagger, which is presented as a new way to combine heterogeneous external knowledge (dependency paths, AMR, knowledge-graph relations) with RNNs. This clearly differentiates it from prior feature-enrichment and tree-based models (e.g., Tree-RNN) and constitutes a non-trivial extension by learning attention over knowledge-derived substructures and feeding a knowledge-guided sentence representation to an RNN-GRU tagger. The demonstrated efficiency and improved generalization on ATIS further support that the approach is meaningfully new rather than a minor combination of known techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes K-SAN, which (per the summary) is the first neural approach to incorporate general prior knowledge (e.g., dependency paths or AMR subgraphs) via an attention mechanism in an end-to-end model, clearly differentiating it from standard RNN/Tree-RNN/CNN baselines. Encoding knowledge-guided substructures, computing attention over them, and feeding the resulting representation to an RNN-GRU is a non-trivial extension beyond existing sequence taggers, and would likely provide new insight to experts working on slot-filling despite the underlying task being well-studied.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—integrating general prior knowledge into end-to-end neural models for NLU—by proposing K-SAN, which uses structural attention over knowledge-guided substructures and can incorporate diverse knowledge types (dependency paths, KB relations, AMR) and is claimed to be efficient and parallelizable. Empirically it improves generalization from small training data and outperforms prior neural taggers on ATIS slot filling, indicating practical value and likely influence on how researchers integrate external knowledge. While evaluation is confined to ATIS (limited domain), the general framework and attention-based integration suggest strong potential to open new research directions and be adopted in related sequence-labeling and NLU tasks. | B(4): The paper tackles an important problem—integrating general prior knowledge into end-to-end neural NLU—by proposing K-SAN, which uses structural attention to incorporate diverse knowledge sources (dependency paths, AMR, knowledge graphs) and thereby generalizes RNN taggers. Empirically it demonstrates improved generalization from small training sets and outperforms prior neural slot-filling models on ATIS, suggesting it could change how researchers incorporate external structure into sequence models. This approach opens clear new directions for knowledge-guided architectures and is likely to be built upon, though its demonstrated impact is currently constrained by evaluation primarily on ATIS.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—integrating general prior knowledge into end-to-end neural models for NLU—by proposing K-SAN, which uses structural attention over knowledge-guided substructures and can incorporate diverse knowledge types (dependency paths, KB relations, AMR) and is claimed to be efficient and parallelizable. Empirically it improves generalization from small training data and outperforms prior neural taggers on ATIS slot filling, indicating practical value and likely influence on how researchers integrate external knowledge. While evaluation is confined to ATIS (limited domain), the general framework and attention-based integration suggest strong potential to open new research directions and be adopted in related sequence-labeling and NLU tasks.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (CRF Tagger, RNN Tagger, CNN Encoder-Tagger, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on the same ATIS data using F-measure, and reports hyperparameters, suggesting fair experimental conditions. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the methodological differences (knowledge-guided substructures and attention) are explained. However, the summary does not indicate explicit acknowledgement when prior methods perform comparably or better, so there is a minor omission in comparative discussion. | B(4): The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on the standard ATIS slot-filling task using F-measure, indicating fair experimental conditions and metrics. Related work coverage is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility. The summary does not explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, so there may be a minor omission in reporting comparative nuances. Overall the comparisons are good and appropriate, with only small gaps in explicit acknowledgment/analysis of ties or stronger baselines.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's central claims (knowledge-guided attention, end-to-end training, improved slot-filling on ATIS) are supported by experiments against reasonable strong baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) including small-data splits, which lends credibility to the empirical claims. There are no formal mathematical proofs in the summary, but the model description (encoding substructures, attention, RNN-GRU tagger) is coherent and does not present obvious derivation errors. Methodology is largely sound given appropriate baselines and reported hyperparameters, but the summary omits statistical significance testing and provides no empirical measurements to substantiate the claimed efficiency/parallelization advantages. Also the absolute claim of being the “first” such neural approach is not substantiated in the summary. These are minor issues that do not overturn the main experimental conclusions. | B(4): The main claims (K-SAN architecture, use of knowledge-guided substructures with attention, end-to-end training, and empirical gains on ATIS slot filling) are clearly described and supported by experiments against appropriate baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on ATIS datasets with hyperparameters reported. However, some asserted benefits — e.g., efficiency/parallelizability and being the "first" neural approach to use general prior knowledge — are not shown to have direct experimental verification in the summary, and no statistical significance testing is mentioned. Overall the technical methodology and reported results support the core conclusions, with only minor missing evidence for a few secondary claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's central claims (knowledge-guided attention, end-to-end training, improved slot-filling on ATIS) are supported by experiments against reasonable strong baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) including small-data splits, which lends credibility to the empirical claims. There are no formal mathematical proofs in the summary, but the model description (encoding substructures, attention, RNN-GRU tagger) is coherent and does not present obvious derivation errors. Methodology is largely sound given appropriate baselines and reported hyperparameters, but the summary omits statistical significance testing and provides no empirical measurements to substantiate the claimed efficiency/parallelization advantages. Also the absolute claim of being the “first” such neural approach is not substantiated in the summary. These are minor issues that do not overturn the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (K-SAN architecture, use of knowledge-guided substructures with attention, end-to-end training, and empirical gains on ATIS slot filling) are clearly described and supported by experiments against appropriate baselines (CRF, RNN, CNN, DCNN, Tree-RNN) on ATIS datasets with hyperparameters reported. However, some asserted benefits — e.g., efficiency/parallelizability and being the "first" neural approach to use general prior knowledge — are not shown to have direct experimental verification in the summary, and no statistical significance testing is mentioned. Overall the technical methodology and reported results support the core conclusions, with only minor missing evidence for a few secondary claims.

main_claims: Introduces K-SAN, the first neural network approach (claimed) that utilizes general prior knowledge as guidance in an end-to-end fashion with an attention mechanism to learn important substructures.

K-SAN generalizes RNNs to incorporate different types of external knowledge (no required schema), e.g., dependency relations, knowledge-graph relations, or handcrafted-grammar parses, to guide structural attention.
K-SAN is efficient and parallelizable because substructures from the input utterance are modeled separately, so modeling time may not increase linearly with sentence length.

K-SAN can capture salient substructures from small training data, thereby improving generalization to previously unseen sequences and outperforming state-of-the-art neural NLU taggers on ATIS (slot filling).
_summary.methodology: K-SAN encodes knowledge-guided substructures (e.g., dependency parse root-to-leaf paths or AMR subgraphs) into vector representations, computes attention weights over these substructures to form a knowledge-guided sentence representation, and feeds that representation into an RNN-GRU tagger for sequence (slot) labeling in an end-to-end trained model.
datasets: ATIS (including ATIS-2 and ATIS-3 subsets; training: 4978 utterances from Class A of ATIS-2/ATIS-3; 893 utterances from ATIS-3 Nov93/Dec94)
limitations: For feature-enrichment based prior approaches the paper states possible limitations: poor generalization and error propagation.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - natural language understanding (slot filling / sequence tagging)
